articles.title: Final Approval Granted for 158-Home Development in Castleton
articles.excerpt: Rochdale council has granted final approval for a 158-home development in Castleton, allowing construction to begin on the affordable housing scheme.
articles.content_html:
A 158-home development in Castleton has received final approval from Rochdale council, enabling construction to commence on the affordable housing scheme. The project will feature a mix of two and three-bedroom houses built on Trub Farm, located off Manchester Road.

MCI Developments will transform the land, which is bounded by the canal and situated near Castleton town centre, into a residential area. The plans include private gardens, a children’s play park, and public open space. All homes will be offered at an affordable rate, priced at up to 80 per cent of market value.

The development will consist primarily of semi-detached houses and small terraces, with 48 apartments spread across two three-storey blocks. Access to the site will be via Trub Road, featuring a bridge and walkway over the canal to connect pedestrians to the existing canal footpath. A proposed stairway to the north of the site will also facilitate easy access to Castleton.

The planning committee initially approved the scheme in July 2025, but the local authority has only recently finalised details with the developer regarding the management of public spaces and financial contributions to local infrastructure. Planning documents describe the project as a well-designed neighbourhood that will enhance the area and its surroundings.

articles.title: Rochdale Council Proposes New Dog Fouling Public Space Protection Order
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Borough Council has proposed a new Public Space Protection Order aimed at tackling dog fouling in the area, following concerns over cleanliness and public health.
articles.content_html:
Rochdale Borough Council has proposed a new Public Space Protection Order (PSPO) to address dog fouling in the area. The initiative aims to improve cleanliness and public health.

The proposed PSPO is in accordance with the Anti-Social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014, Section 59. It seeks to provide the council with additional powers to manage dog fouling effectively.

If implemented, the order could lead to increased fines for dog owners who fail to clean up after their pets.

The council is currently seeking feedback on the proposal, with further details available in the document published on their website.

articles.title: Rochdale Council to Introduce Tighter Controls on HMOs
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. Reform councillors have called on town hall officials to implement an Article 4 Direction to address growing tensions surrounding HMOs. This decision follows protests against HMOs that took place over the weekend in Heywood. Councillor Paul O’Neill…
articles.content_html:
Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. Reform councillors have called on town hall officials to implement an Article 4 Direction to address growing tensions surrounding HMOs. This decision follows protests against HMOs that took place over the weekend in Heywood.

Councillor Paul O’Neill stated that the council aims to ease tensions by ensuring that all HMOs, regardless of size, require specific planning permission. Currently, only HMOs accommodating seven or more people need such permission. Reform councillor Lee Wolf highlighted that while HMO licensing is important, it does not regulate their locations or prevent excessive clustering in certain areas.

Residents have expressed a desire for greater input regarding HMOs, with many believing they are negatively impacting local services. The Labour group, which currently controls the council, acknowledged the issue and indicated that they are already planning changes. Housing boss Councillor Danny Meredith thanked Reform for their support of the Labour plan.

Councillor Richard Jackson from Labour added that the council is actively working on this matter but emphasised the need to follow proper processes. He noted that implementing an Article 4 Direction requires substantial evidence and cannot be enacted immediately. The proposal is currently undergoing local authority processes and is expected to be presented to the full council for a formal vote later this year.
